emotional_core: The silence we were taught to mistake for strength is actually brittleness — and breaking it, one honest conversation at a time, is the real act of courage.
visual_metaphor: A person sitting in a well-lit room surrounded by people, completely unreachable — the kind of alone that looks like togetherness from the outside.
key_quotes: Strength that requires silence is just brittleness waiting for the right amount of pressure.
Some moments don't need a mechanic. They need a witness.
Vulnerability isn't the opposite of strength. It's proof of it.

story_frames.visual: A person's arm in a cast surrounded by friends smiling and signing it — warm tones, full social permission, everyone leaning in.
story_frames.text: Break a bone. People show up.

story_frames.visual: The same person alone at a kitchen table late at night, chest tight, empty chair across from them — cold tones, shadow-heavy.
story_frames.text: Say you're drowning. Room goes quiet.

story_frames.visual: Dust-sheeted furniture in an old room — inherited, unused, left in place because nobody ever decided to move it out.
story_frames.text: The stigma is inherited. Not chosen.

story_frames.visual: Two people at a table — one leaning forward mid-sentence with a spreadsheet open on a laptop, the other looking down, disconnected, not quite there.
story_frames.text: Some moments need a witness. Not a mechanic.

story_frames.visual: Clean split frame: a bright gym on the left with an open door and a trainer inside, a therapy office on the right with a closed door and a question mark on the frosted glass.
Two materials under identical pressure — one rigid and fracturing into sharp pieces, one flexible and bending but holding its shape — same size, same color.
A concrete wall with a single crack running through it — warm light bleeding through from the other side, barely visible but unmistakably there.
